justification: All 7 required rules identified (plus 2 bonus rules), 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (well above the 4 minimum), and extensive ambiguities surfaced with a structured clarification table. However, ALL 9 rules are marked invariant: true uniformly — this hits the explicit failure mode 'All rules marked invariant: true uniformly' and misses the core rubric requirement to distinguish soft HR policies (accrual rate, carryover cap, 3-day advance notice, medical retroactive window) from structural domain invariants (negative balance, balance integrity, contract boundary). Accrual rate and carryover cap were specifically required to be invariant: false. The agent never distinguishes labor-law-adjacent rules from company policy rules, which was a key expected behavior.

observations.text: All 9 rules marked invariant: true — no distinction between configurable HR policies and hard domain invariants. Accrual rate (1.5 days/month) is a configurable policy, not a state constraint. Carryover cap (10 days) is HR policy. 3-day advance notice is waivable company policy. Medical retroactive window is HR policy. All should be invariant: false.
No explicit categorization of rules into labor-law-adjacent vs HR policy — the rubric required Bird to distinguish these two classes.
All 7 required rules identified plus 2 additional valid rules (manager-only approval, balance consumed on approval only).
12 detailed Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering accrual, negative balance, medical leave exception, carryover, contract boundary, and advance notice — far exceeding the 4 minimum.

Extensive ambiguities surfaced: partial month accrual, calendar vs working days for medical window, contract end date inclusive/exclusive, planned medical leave notice, concurrent approval race conditions, cross-year approval timing — all formatted as a structured HR clarification table.
Rich ubiquitous language section with 11 well-defined domain terms.
Edge cases section identifies 8 scenarios including race conditions on concurrent approvals and cross-year-boundary approval timing.
Confidence level of 78 is reasonable given the number of unresolved ambiguities, showing good calibration.
Business impact analysis covers financial (balance sheet liability), operational, user experience, and compliance risk dimensions with specific stakeholder mapping.
